segments.s391-g145: که begins an optative/subordinating clause here ('that' in the sense of a wish). بی is the preposition 'without' and directly precedes فره ('farr', divine glory). اورنگ شاهی is a genitive-like nominal phrase 'throne of kingship' (noun + noun), with شاهی specifying the type of throne. مباد is an archaic/poetic negative optative meaning 'may it not be' and closes the clause.
segments.s391-g146: This clause is a coordinated negative wish parallel to the previous one: بزرگی ('greatness') and رسم سپاهی ('custom/tradition of the army') are the coordinated nouns, with سپاهی functioning adjectivally to specify رسم. مباد again negates the whole coordinated phrase as an optative ('may there not be').
segments.s391-g147: برفتند is the past-tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they went/they departed' placed before its subject (a common poetic V–S order). گردان is the noun 'troop' (postverbal subject), while فرخنده رای is a compound-like modifier ('fortunate/auspicious counsel/plan') that qualifies the troop (i.e. 'the troop of/with fortunate counsel').
segments.s391-g148: برابر is an adverbial/qualifying word here meaning 'evenly / in line / equally' and modifies the verb کشیدند ('they drew, pulled, arranged'), which is 3rd-person plural past. پرده‌سرای is the object/locative noun 'pavilion/theatre/curtain-hall' — here the thing they 'drew' or arranged. The whole phrase means they set up or stretched the curtain-hall in an orderly line.
segments.s391-g121: ز (preposition) here means 'because of / by'; پیکان پولاد is a noun + modifier ('arrows of steel' / 'steel arrows'); گشتند is the past plural of گشتن 'to become/turn' with an implied plural subject ('they' — the foes); سست is the predicate adjective 'weak'. The clause literally: 'By the steel arrows they became weak.'
segments.s391-g122: نیامد is past negation 'did not come'; یکی in negative contexts means 'not one / not a single' (indefinite); پیش او = 'before him / in his presence'; تن درست is a noun + adjective 'a sound/intact body' (درست = 'proper, whole'). The clause: 'Not a single sound body came before him.'
segments.s391-g123: نگه کرد is a verbal phrase 'looked / cast his gaze' formed with نگه (gaze) + کرد (past of کردن). روشن‌دل is an epithet 'clear‑hearted / bright‑minded' attached to the name اسفندیار, and the name follows the epithet here. Word order is verb → epithet + name.
segments.s391-g124: بدید is the past ('he saw'); آن‌که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that/what'; دد سست is a noun + adjective 'the (hostile) enemy weak'; برگشت کار is an idiomatic past construction 'the work/affair turned' i.e., 'the tide/relation of the fight was reversed.' The verbs are simple past, describing what he perceived.
segments.s391-g125: یکی is an indefinite 'someone / a certain one' introducing an actor; تیغ 'sword' and زهرآبگون 'poison‑green' form a noun + descriptive compound; برکشید is the past 'drew up / drew (a weapon)'. The clause means 'a certain one drew a poison‑tinted sword.'
segments.s391-g126: عنان را گران کرد is an idiom: عنان 'the reins' + را (definite object marker) + گران کرد 'made heavy' → 'he tightened/checked the reins' (slowed the horse). The conjunction و links the next action; سر درکشید uses درکشید here in the physical sense 'pulled/drew in (up)' with سر 'head' as object, i.e. 'he lifted/drew up his head.'
segments.s391-g127: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout / all over'; به شمشیرشان marks the instrument 'with their sword' (به used instrumentally); کرد is the past 'made/did'; چاک is the noun 'a gash / slit' that is produced. Word order places the adverb and instrument before the verb and its object.
segments.s391-g128: گل انگیخت is a poetic intransitive/eventive phrase 'a flower sprang up' or 'flowers arose'; انگیخت (past of انگیختن) here carries the sense 'to cause/bring forth / to spring up.' از خون ایشان ز خاک stacks two 'from' phrases: 'from their blood' (از خون ایشان) and 'out of the earth' (ز خاک), a poetic inversion meaning 'flowers sprang up out of the earth from their blood.'
segments.s391-g129: فرود آمد is a past verbal phrase meaning 'alighted / came down' (فرود as 'fall/alighting' + آمد 'came'); از introduces the source ('from the ...') and governs the following noun phrase نامور بارگی, where نامور is an adjective 'famed/renowned' modifying بارگی 'foliage/leaf'. Word order here is typical poetic Persian: verb followed by a prepositional source phrase.
segments.s391-g130: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'to God' (به + یزدان). نمود is an archaic/poetic past form of نمودن 'show / make known' used here as 'he showed/made known'; او is the post‑verbal subject 'he' (common in poetry), and ز بیچارگی is an ablative/cause phrase 'out of/through misery' modifying نمود. Overall: 'to God he showed (himself) from misery.'
segments.s391-g131: This clause lists the objects before the verb: سلیح و تن 'weapon and body' (two coordinated nouns). از خون ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'from/in their blood' indicating the medium/means, and بشست is the past transitive verb 'he washed' (he cleaned/wiped). The construction means 'he washed his weapon and body in their blood.'
segments.s391-g132: بر آن خارستان پاک جایی is a prepositional phrase 'onto/onto that thorny grove [a] clean place' where آن determines خارستان and پاک qualifies جایی ('a clear/clean spot'). بجست is the past verb 'he leapt / sprang' (ب- prefix + جست), placed at the end as usual; literal order: 'onto that thorny-grove clean spot he leapt.'
segments.s391-g133: پر here functions as 'full (of)' modifying the following noun phrase آژنگ رخ ('a face full of adornment/brightness'); رخ is the (understood) subject 'his face'. سوی خورشید is a directional phrase 'toward the sun' and کرد is the light/simple past verb 'made/did', giving the idiom 'he turned his face toward the sun.' The sequence is poetic: modifier + subject + direction + verb.
segments.s391-g134: This is a parallel, coordinate description using the 'N پر ز X' pattern meaning 'N full of X': دلی پر ز درد 'a heart full of pain' and سری پر ز گرد 'and a head full of dust.' دلی and سری are nouns ('heart' and 'head'), پر ز introduces the contents ('full of'), درد is emotional/physical 'pain,' and گرد here means 'dust'. The two phrases are joined by و.
segments.s391-g135: همی گفت is an iterative/progressive past idiom meaning 'he kept saying' or 'he said repeatedly' (همی adding continuity). کای is the poetic vocative 'O' and addresses داور دادگر: داور 'judge/arbiter' and دادگر 'just' form a vocative apposition 'O just Judge.' The whole segment is a direct address introduced by the repeated-speech verb.
segments.s391-g136: تو دادی is a simple past clause 'you gave' (pronoun تو 'you' + past verb دادی). م + را in the data represent the object مرا ('me' + object marker را) — together marking the first‑person object. The sequence هوش و زور و هنر lists the gifts in coordination ('intellect, strength, and skill'). The verb precedes its coordinated objects with the object marker following the pronoun.
segments.s391-g97: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. درگاه (here) is 'the royal court/presence' placed after the preposition. برخاست is the past verb 'rose' (subject follows or is implied). آوای کوس is a genitive phrase 'the sound/voice of the drum' where آوای (voice/sound) governs the noun کوس (war-drum). Word order is typical poetry: source (ز درگاه) + verb (برخاست) + what rose (آوای کوس).
segments.s391-g98: زمین آهنین شد سپهر آبنوس is a compact poetic clause: زمین (the earth) + adjective آهنین (iron/steely) + شد (became). The second noun–adjective pair سپهر آبنوس ('the sky, ebony') is coordinated without repeating شد, so the verb 'became' applies to both parts (elliptical construction). Adjectives follow the nouns they modify, as usual in Persian poetry.
segments.s391-g99: سوی is the preposition 'toward'. هفتخوان ('the Seven‑Gates/Seven‑Trials') is placed after it as the directional object. رخ به توران نهاد is an idiomatic sequence: رخ 'face' + به 'toward' + توران 'Turan' + نهاد (past of نهادن) 'set/placed' — idiomatically 'set his face toward Turan' (= 'turned toward Turan'). Note the past verb نهاد governs the preceding nominal phrase.
segments.s391-g100: همی رفت uses همی as a poetic/progressive particle ('was going/kept going') plus the past verb رفت 'went/marched'. با لشکر introduces the accompaniment 'with the army/host'. آباد و شاد are two post-nominal adjectives modifying لشکر ('prosperous and joyful'); the coordinating و links them. Overall: 'he marched with a prosperous, joyful army.'
segments.s391-g101: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از راه 'from the road' gives the route; نزدیک is 'near' and here qualifies منزل (the lodging/stage) that follows. منزل رسید is the verb phrase 'reached (the) station/lodging' with رسید the past tense 'arrived/reached'. The order places the temporal clause first: 'When (he) from the road reached the nearby station...'.
segments.s391-g102: ز لشکر 'from the army' uses ز 'from' to mark the source. یکی نامور is a noun phrase 'a certain/one renowned (man)', where یکی = 'one/a certain' and نامور = 'renowned'. برگزید is the past verb 'chose/selected'. The construction means 'he chose a renowned one from the host'; note there is no object marker را here because the 'ز ...' source frame is used.
segments.s391-g103: پشوتن is the proper name ('Peshotan'). یکی مرد بیدار is a predicate phrase 'a certain/one man alert/vigilant' with یکی 'a certain' and مرد 'man' + adjective بیدار 'awake/alert'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole gives 'Peshotan was an alert man.'
segments.s391-g104: سپه را uses را to topicalize/emphasize 'the army' as the thing affected. ز دشمن ('from/against the enemy') indicates source or adversary—here 'against the enemy'. نگهدار is a noun 'guardian/keeper'. بود is again the past copula 'was'. The clause reads 'he was the army's guardian against the enemy,' with را marking سپه as the definite/topic element.
segments.s391-g73: بدو گفت = “he said to him”: the directional pronoun بدو precedes the verb گفت and marks the indirect object (‘to him’). The clause ما را جز این راه نیست is an existential/impersonal construction: راه نیست is the predicate ‘there is no way’ and ما را (with the object-marking را) marks the affected party (‘for us’). جز این = ‘except this’.
segments.s391-g74: به گیتی = a locative phrase ‘in the world’ (poetic word order). The sequence به از + noun is an archaic comparative/conventional formula here: به از راه کوتاه نیست = ‘there is no shorter/better way’ (نیست negates existence of such a way). Adjectival کوتاه follows راه; the verb نیست applies to راه.
segments.s391-g75: چنین گفت = ‘thus he said/so he spoke’ (چنین as adverb). با + epithet (با نامور گرگسار) indicates the addressee or the one being described — here the phrase names the famous person (نامور) with the epithet گرگسار; گفت is past tense ‘said’.
segments.s391-g76: که introduces the reported content/continuation of speech (‘that…’). این هفتخوان = ‘these Seven Trials’ (a fixed mythic phrase). هرگز‌ای شهریار = ‘never, O king’ — here هرگز is the negator/ adverb ‘never’ and ای شهریار is a vocative (‘O king’) placed after it for emphasis; the clause is left incomplete and continues in the following lines.
segments.s391-g77: به زور و به آواز = two adverbial phrases using به + noun to form ‘by force and by voice/cry’ (instrumental/means). نگذشت کس = ‘no one passed’ (نگذشت = past negative of گذشت ‘passed’; کس here functions as ‘anyone/no one’ with the negative verb). Word order places the means before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s391-g78: مگر introduces the exception ‘except’. کز is the contracted form of که از / که‌ز and here acts as a relative ‘who/which’ or ‘one who’. تن خویش = ‘his own body’ (خویش split into خوی + ش: خوی is the root, ش = possessive suffix ‘his’). کردست بس is an archaic perfective phrase ‘has done enough’ (کردست = ‘has done/has made’, بس = ‘enough’), i.e. ‘except one who has sufficiently given of his body’.
segments.s391-g79: بدو = ‘to him’ (indirect object) placed before the verb; نامور گفت‌گر = ‘the famed speaker’ (an epithet + agent noun). با منی = ‘you are with me’ — با + pronoun من plus the enclitic copula ی (here marking second person singular ‘are’). The overall phrase is an introductory indirect-address + speech verb.
segments.s391-g80: ببینی is a second‑person singular present/subjunctive/optative form ‘you will see’ / ‘may you see’ (used here to forecast or promise). دل و زور آهرمنی = ‘the heart and might/strength of Ahriman (demonic)’, with آهرمنی as an adjective meaning ‘Ahrimanic’ or ‘demonic’; the conjunction و links دل and زور as a compound object of ببینی.
segments.s391-g33: Word order is poetic: the preposition ز + pronoun من ('from me') comes first, then the verb نشنود ('hears' with negative prefix in classical style) and the subject شاه ('the king') follows the verb. جز introduces the exception, and گفت راست is a noun phrase 'utterance/words (گفت)' qualified by راست ('true'). The overall sense is 'the king hears from me nothing except true words.'
segments.s391-g34: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. آن کن is an imperative construction meaning 'do that' (آن = 'that', کن = 'do!'). که introduces a relative clause ('that/which'), and از پادشاهی سزاست is a predicative phrase: از marks the relation 'of/for', پادشاهی = 'kingship', and سزاست is a present copular/predicative form meaning 'it is fitting/merited'. The clause thus commands: 'Do what is fitting for kingship.'
segments.s391-g35: بدو is a dative/indirect-object clitic 'to him'. گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'he said' (classical phrasing). رویین دژ is an adjective + noun ('iron/iron-clad fortress'), with the adjective preceding the noun as common. اکنون کجاست is an interrogative clause ('where is it now?') formed with interrogative کجا + copula ست.
segments.s391-g36: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('since/that'), and the clause follows normal order: آن مرز ('that border') as subject, از این بوم ایران as a prepositional phrase 'from this land of Iran', and جداست as the predicate 'is separate/has been severed'. The clause qualifies/grounds the question about the fortress.
segments.s391-g37: بدو again 'to him'. چند راهست is an interrogative phrase 'how many ways/roads are there?' with چند (how many) preceding the noun راهست (way/path). و فرسنگ چند repeats the question for distance: 'and how many parasangs?' The connective و simply links the two parallel questions.
segments.s391-g38: کدام آنک 'which one of those' uses the archaic demonstrative/relative آنک. ازو ('of/from it/them') marks the domain being asked about; هست is the copula 'is'. بیم و گزند is a fixed pair meaning 'fear and harm' or 'risk and injury' — i.e., 'which of them is exposed to danger.'
segments.s391-g39: سپه چند باشد is another interrogative: 'how many troops are there (may be)?' باشد is a classical present/subjunctive/copular form used poetically for existence. همیشه 'always' modifies the verb phrase, and در اوی ('in it') uses the archaic oblique pronoun اوی for 'in it/inside it'.
segments.s391-g40: ز بالای دژ is a prepositional phrase 'from the top of the fortress' (ز = 'from', بالای = 'above/on top of', دژ = 'fortress'). هرچه دانی is a free relative 'whatever you know' with دانی 'you know' (second-person present/subjunctive). بگوی is an imperative/request 'speak/tell' (second-person), so the clause requests that he report whatever he knows from the fortress top.
segments.s391-g41: Literal word order is inverted poetically: 'چنین داد پاسخ ورا' = 'thus gave answer to him'. 'داد' is the simple past of دادن (gave) and with 'پاسخ' forms the verb phrase 'پاسخ داد' = 'answered'. 'ورا' is the enclitic compound of 'به + او' (to him/unto him). 'گرگسار' follows as an epithet applied to the speaker (the 'wolfish/ferocious one').
segments.s391-g42: 'که‌ای' here functions as an archaic/vocative particle (equivalent to 'که ای' = 'O') used to address someone directly. 'شیردل' is an epithet 'lion‑hearted'; 'خسرو' is the proper name Khosrow; 'شهریار' is a title 'king/sovereign'. The three words form a direct address to the ruler.
segments.s391-g43: 'سه راهست' = 'three routes exist' (راه + است contracted to راهست); 'ز' is the poetic preposition = 'from'. 'ایدر' means 'here'. 'بدان' is the fusion of 'به + آن' = 'to that' (a poetic orthography). 'شارستان' means 'city/town.' The phrase as a whole states number and origin/destination: 'three routes from here to that city.'
segments.s391-g44: 'که' is the relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing a clause giving a name; 'ارجاسپ' is the proper name Arjasp. 'خواندش' is the preterite 'خواند' (called/read) plus the enclitic object 'ش' = 'it/him', so 'he called it/named it'. 'پیکارستان' is the noun 'battlefield' and is the name he gives.
segments.s391-g45: The pattern 'یکی ... و یکی ...' means 'one ... and another ...' with the copular/lexical verb omitted (understood from context) and so denotes durations: 'one (takes) three months and one (takes) two months.' 'در X ماه' here expresses the time-span 'in X months.'
segments.s391-g46: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'. 'ایدون' is an older/poetic form of آمدن 'to come/arrive' (here 'if the coming/advance ...'). 'خورش' in this classical/poetic context refers to the sun/sunlight (a literary contraction of خورشید) rather than the modern cooking sense—this sense is not in the provided list. 'تنگ باشد' uses 'تنگ' = 'narrow, constricted' plus 'باشد' (subjunctive/optative form) to mark the hypothetical: 'if the sun's passage is constricted on the road.' 'به راه' = 'on the way/road.'
segments.s391-g47: 'گیا هست' = 'there is vegetation/grass' (noun + copula). 'و' = 'and'. 'آبشخور چارپای' = 'a watering-place for the quadrupeds' (poetic omission of the ezāfe; the noun 'چارپای' qualifies 'آبشخور'). Together they state that fodder and water exist for animals.
segments.s391-g48: 'فرود آمدن' is the infinitive noun 'to descend / to alight' (i.e., to dismount). The object marker 'را' marks that infinitive as the direct object of 'نیابی'. 'نیابی' is the negative present/subjunctive form of 'یافتن' (you do not find / you will not find) with 'تو' as the explicit subject pronoun. 'جای' = 'a place'. The clause means 'you will not find a place to dismount.'
segments.s391-g25: This clause states refusal or negation of harming something. 'نیازارم' is an archaic negative verb phrase here meaning 'I will not harm / I will not trouble' (negative-prefixed form of آزارم); 'آن را' is a demonstrative ('that') plus the definite/object marker 'را' marking the direct object. 'که' introduces a relative clause 'that/which', and 'پیوند' is the head noun 'bond/link' of that relative clause. The final word 'تست' is an archaic form (here functioning as a 2nd-person copula/possessive 'is yours' or indicates relation to 'you') rather than the modern verb 'to test'.
segments.s391-g26: Parallel construction introduced by 'هم' ('also' / 'too') repeating the same object-form structure: 'هم آن را' ('also that [object]'), 'که' again introduces a relative clause modifying that object, 'پیوند فرزند' = 'the bond of the child/descendant'. The final 'تست' again is the archaic second-person copula/possessive marker ('is yours' / 'of you') that connects the bond to the addressee's child.
segments.s391-g27: 'وگر' is a conditional conjunction 'if / but if'. 'هیچ' here means 'no / not a single', modifying 'گردی' which in this context means a tiny particle or speck (literally 'dust'). 'به گرد' functions as a locative/relational phrase 'into/around the circle (or orbit) of ...' where 'گرد' means 'around/about' and takes 'دروغ' 'lie/falsehood' as its complement: 'into/around the circle of falsehood'.
segments.s391-g28: This is the result clause: 'نگیرد' is a negative/subjunctive or jussive form of 'گرفتن' meaning 'may not take / may not seize' (here rendered as 'may ... not seize'). 'بر من' = 'upon me'. 'دروغت' is 'your lie' (دروغ + possessive -ت). 'فروغ' is a poetic noun 'radiance / brightness' describing the lie. The sequence means roughly 'may the radiance of your lie not seize/take hold upon me.'
segments.s391-g29: 'میانت' (from میان) means 'in your middle / between you' and here marks the target location: 'I will [place] my dagger between you' — practically 'I will split you down the middle.' 'به خنجر کنم' uses the verb 'کنم' (present/subjunctive/intentional 'I will do / I will make') with 'به خنجر' 'with a dagger' as instrument; final 'به دو نیم' is the goal/result 'into two halves.' Word order is verb-final in the subordinate/action clause typical of Persian poetry, but 'کنم' is first-person present/future-intentional.
segments.s391-g30: 'دل انجمن گردد' is a nominal phrase with verb 'گردد' (from گشت/گرد) meaning 'become' or 'turn'; here 'the heart of the assembly will become ...' 'از تو' = 'because of you / on account of you', and 'به بیم' means 'into fear' (بیم = fear). The whole clause describes the consequence: the assembly's heart will be turned to fear because of you.
segments.s391-g31: 'چنین داد پاسخ ورا' is a reporting clause: 'چنین' = 'thus', 'داد' = past tense 'gave' (here 'gave an answer'), 'پاسخ' = 'answer/reply' the direct object, and 'ورا' (to + را) = 'to him' — so 'thus gave the reply to him.' 'گرگسار' follows as the speaker's epithet/name (used appositionally here).
segments.s391-g32: This is the vocative/exclamatory reply quoted: 'که‌ای' is an archaic vocative/exclamatory particle (here introducing an address: 'O ...'), 'نامور' = 'renowned', 'فرخ' = 'fortunate/blessed', followed by the proper name 'اسفندیار'. Together the words address Esfandiyār with flattering epithets.
segments.s391-g17: بفرمود تا is a past, honorific verb plus the subordinating تا meaning “that/so that” introducing the commanded action. جام زرین چهار is a poetic, inverted noun phrase: جام زرین = “golden cup,” and the numeral چهار follows for metrical reasons (so read as “four golden cups”).
segments.s391-g18: دمادم is an adverb “constantly/continually.” ببستند is a 3rd-person plural verb form here used in the jussive/command clause introduced by تا (so: “they should bind/tie” or simply “they bound/they tied” in narrative). بر is the preposition “on/upon,” and گرگسار is the person/place affected (here a name or epithet).
segments.s391-g19: از آن پس is a time phrase “from that time / after that.” بدو گفت = “he said to him” (بدو = to him; گفت is the speech verb). کای is the vocative particle “O,” and تیره‑بخت is the address “ill‑fated” or “unfortunate one.”
segments.s391-g20: رسانم is a 1st‑person verb “I will bring / I will cause to reach.” The element ت marks the second‑person object (you) as an enclitic, represented here separately as ترا (the accusative/emphatic form “you”). من is an emphatic subject “I” placed after the verb for emphasis. به تاج و به تخت is a doubled prepositional phrase “to the crown and to the throne,” with repeated به for rhetorical balance.
segments.s391-g21: This is a conditional subordinate clause. 'گر' is the conditional particle 'if'; 'ایدون' is an archaic verb meaning 'come/occur' used impersonally ('if it comes to pass'); 'که' introduces the content clause; 'هرچت' (= هرچه) means 'whatever'; 'بپرسیم' is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/optative ('we ask' or 'we should ask'), marked by the b- prefix; and 'راست' is an adverb 'truthfully' that grammatically belongs with the following verb (it modifies 'بگویی' in the next segment). The word order places the conditional and subordinate clause before the main verb of the sentence, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s391-g22: 'بگویی' here is the present/subjunctive form meaning 'you speak/say' (used in the apodosis of the conditional: 'you speak/answer'); it is an archaic/poetic verb form with the b- prefix. 'همه شهر ترکان' is the noun phrase 'the whole city of the Turks' (همه = all/entire; شهر = city; ترکان = the Turks). 'تراست' is a compound predicate meaning 'is yours' (ترا = to you / you (object); است = is), here fused as the fixed poetic form 'تراست' = 'belongs to you / is yours'. The clause states the result: if the condition holds, then the city will be yours.
segments.s391-g23: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; 'پیروز' is the adjective 'victorious'; 'گردم' is an archaic/poetic 1st-person-singular present/subjunctive of گشتن meaning 'I become' ('چو پیروز گردم' = 'when I become victorious'). 'سپارم' is a 1st-person-singular present-subjunctive/intention form of سپردن, 'I will entrust' or 'I will give over'; 'ت' is the 2nd-person object clitic 'you' and 'را' is the definite-object marker. The clause therefore gives a temporal condition followed by a promise to entrust the addressee.
segments.s391-g24: 'به خورشید تابان' is a prepositional phrase 'to the shining sun' with تابان post‑modifying خورشید. 'برآرم' is a 1st-person-singular verb 'I will lift up / I will raise' (poetic form with the prefix بر- and -م ending), and 'ترا' (ت + را) repeats the object 'you' with the object marker. Grammatically the second verb shares the same object and continues the promise from the previous clause; the lack of an explicit conjunction is normal in verse, and both verbs are first-person declarations about what the speaker will do to the addressee.
segments.s391-g1: سخن‌گوی دهقان is a compound/construct: سخن‌گو ('one who speaks') modified by دهقان (the peasant), so 'the peasant's speaker' or 'the peasant who spoke'. چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as', بنهاد is 3rd person past of بنهادن 'placed/set', and خوان here is the direct object 'feast/table'. Word order is Subject (سخن‌گوی دهقان) + temporal subordinator (چو) + verb (بنهاد) + object (خوان).
segments.s391-g2: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing one tale). داستان is the object 'story/tale' placed before the verb (typical poetic order). راند is past 3rd-person of راندن used figuratively here ('drove forth' → 'related/told'). از marks source/topic ('from/of') and هفتخوان names the subject-matter 'the Seven Trials'.
segments.s391-g3: ز (poetic) introduces the topical/genitive phrase 'about/from'. رویین دژ is an adjectival compound 'iron-hard fortress' (adjective before noun). و links the next noun phrase; کار اسفندیار is 'the deeds/work of Esfandiyar' with کار as the head and اسفندیار in apposition/genitive.
segments.s391-g4: The phrase repeats ز to mark a new topical phrase: 'ز راه' = 'about/from the road'. و connects the next phrase. ز آموزش گرگسار = 'about/from the instruction/training of (the) wolf‑haunted (places)' — here آموزش (instruction/training) is the noun head followed by the adjective/descriptor گرگسار ('wolf‑infested/ferocious'), an adjective placed after its noun in this poetic order.
segments.s391-g5: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت here is the verb 'said' (past 3rd‑singular) and is followed by the relative/archaic particle کو meaning 'who' (introducing the relative clause). چون begins a subordinate temporal clause 'when', بیامد is past 'he came/arrived', به is the preposition 'to', and بلخ is the place. The structure is 'Thus said he who, when he came to Balkh ...'.
segments.s391-g6: زبان و روان is a coordinated nominal phrase 'tongue and soul/spirit'. پر here is an adjectival predicate meaning 'full (of)' and is followed by ز introducing the content 'of'. گفتار تلخ = 'bitter speech/utterance' where تلخ modifies گفتار. Word order is Noun(s) + predicate (پر ز + noun phrase).
segments.s391-g7: همی is a verbal particle indicating ongoing or habitual action ('kept, was continuously'). راند is past of راندن ('drove/rode/continued on'); تا introduces the temporal limit 'until'. پیشش آمد is an inverted clause where آمد (came/appeared) precedes the subject دو راه ('two roads') which is the thing that 'came before him' (پیشش = before him).
segments.s391-g8: سراپرده و خیمه are coordinated objects ('inner curtains/inner-chamber curtains and tents'). زد is the past 3rd‑singular of زدن and here means 'set up/put up/pitched' (a flexible poetic use of زدن). با سپاه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the army/host'. Overall: he pitched curtains and tents with his army.
segments.s391-g81: 'به پیشم' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before me / in my presence' placed before the verb for emphasis. 'چه گویی' is the 2nd-person singular present of گفتن (here rhetorical): 'what do you say'. The clause ends with 'چه آید نخست' where 'چه' = 'what', 'آید' (present/impersonal/future sense) = 'comes/happens', and 'نخست' = 'first' — the sequence of two 'چه' + verb constructions is a poetic repetitive question ('what do you say' / 'what will come first').
segments.s391-g82: 'که' introduces the content/explanatory clause ('that...'). 'باید' is a modal verb meaning 'must / it is necessary'. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from / in (the matter of)' introducing the object of necessity ('in fighting'). 'پیکار' is the noun 'battle / fight'. 'او' is the 3rd-person pronoun 'him'. 'راه' here means 'way / method', and 'جست' is the infinitive 'to seek' (classical form); together 'راه جست' = 'to seek a way'.
segments.s391-g83: 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus/so'. 'داد' is the past tense 'gave' (here the verb of uttering). 'پاسخ' is the direct object 'answer/reply'. 'ورا' = 'to him' (the recipient). 'گرگسار' is an epithet placed after the verb: poetically it characterises the speaker as 'wolf‑like' or 'ferocious' (an epithet/appositive); word order is emphatic/poetic with agent/epithet following the verb.
segments.s391-g84: This is a direct address. 'که‌ای' (here read as the vocative particle 'که، ای' / 'O') introduces the vocative. 'نامور' modifies 'مرد' = 'renowned man'. 'ناباک' here means 'chaste / morally pure / virtuous'. 'دار' is an archaic/poetic imperative/verb form meaning 'have/hold/keep' (from داشتن) instructing the addressee to keep/maintain that quality — thus the phrase functions as an exhortation 'O famed man, keep (thy) purity'.
segments.s391-g85: 'نخستین' = 'first' (temporal/sequence). 'به پیش تو' is a prepositional phrase 'before you / in front of you' with the possessor/target 'تو' after 'پیش'. 'آید' is the present/future 'comes' and the plural subject 'دو گرگ' comes after the verb (poetic inversion); the verb form remains the classical impersonal/3rd-singular 'آید' while meaning 'come (they)'.
segments.s391-g86: 'نر و ماده' lists 'male and female'. 'هریک' = 'each (one)'. 'چو' = 'like / as' introduces a simile. 'پیلی سترگ' is a compound simile: 'پیلی' = 'elephantine / like an elephant' and 'سترگ' (here adjectival/poetic) conveys the sense of great/striking (literally 'sight/eye'); together the phrase describes each wolf as elephant‑sized/elephant‑like. Word order places the simile after the items it qualifies.
segments.s391-g87: 'دو دندان' = 'two teeth / two fangs' (here 'dental' fangs/tusks). 'به کردار' = 'in the fashion/manner/appearance of' (کردار used poetically as 'manner/form'). 'پیل' = 'elephant' used figuratively to characterize the form of the tusks. 'ژیان' is an archaic/poetic element attached to 'پیل' (literally 'living/life' or 'way of life') and here helps form the compound 'پیل ژیان' = 'elephant-like'. The whole phrase means 'two tusks shaped like an elephant's'.
segments.s391-g88: 'بر' here is a locative/positional particle (poetic use) introducing the following body-part clause; 'و' = 'and'. 'کتف' = 'shoulder / upper flank'. 'فربه' = 'fat / fleshy' describes the shoulder. The second 'و' links 'فربه' with 'لاغر' = 'lean / thin' describing another adjacent part; 'میان' = 'between'. The clause paints a contrastive picture: (there are) a fat shoulder and a lean middle between them.
segments.s391-g9: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning “he commanded/said” (3rd person past). تا introduces a目的/ result clause here ("so that / that"); خوان is the object meaning “the feast/table”; بیاراستند is past plural (they) + verbal stem بیاراستن ‘to arrange/decorate’, so together تا خوان بیاراستند = “that they set/arrayed the table.” Word order is verb (بفرمود) + subordinate clause (تا + verb at end).
segments.s391-g10: می‌و رود و رامشگران خواستند is a coordination of items brought/asked for. می‌و here functions orthographically like می + و (’wine’ + ‘and’), but the provided lemma senses do not list the wine sense, so the sense index is unknown. رود here is the musical instrument (rūd, “lute/string instrument”). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” رامشگران is a plural noun (literally ‘those who calm/comfort’) used here as ‘entertainers/minstrels’, and خواستند is past plural “they asked/desired/asked for.” The verb خواستند governs the coordinated list of things requested.
segments.s391-g11: برفتند is a past plural verb meaning “they departed/they went off.” گردان لشکر is a nominal phrase where گردان (troop/unit) modifies لشکر (army/host) — i.e. ‘the army’s divisions/troops.’ همه follows for emphasis: “all” / “the whole (of them).” The verb precedes its noun phrase here (poetic/archaic ordering) but the meaning is simple past transitive/intransitive action.
segments.s391-g12: نشستند is the past plural of نشستن and here means “they sat down” (the supplied senses list is for a different lemma form, so no matching sense is given). بر is the preposition “on/at/upon” governing خوان “feast/table.” شاه رمه is a genitive/attributive sequence: شاه “king” + رمه “multitude/throng,” i.e. the people at the king’s table — so بر خوان شاه رمه = “at the king’s table/feast.”
segments.s391-g13: یکی here is an indefinite pronoun “a certain / someone.” جام زرین is a noun + adjective (“a golden cup”). به + کف forms the prepositional phrase “into/into the palm” (به with the sense ‘towards/into’). برگرفت is a past tense verb (3rd singular) from برگرفتن “to take up/raise,” so the clause means “someone raised/took up a golden cup in his palm.”
segments.s391-g14: ز is the poetic preposition “from” (here introducing the agent/source of the next action), گشتاسپ is the proper name Gushtasp, آنگه is the temporal adverb “then,” سخن is “speech/words,” and برگرفت (same verb) is used idiomatically “took up speech” = “began to speak.” The sequence therefore means “then Gushtasp took up speech” i.e. “Gushtasp then spoke.”
segments.s391-g15: وز آن پس is a conjunctive temporal phrase equivalent to و از آن پس “and after that / thereafter.” (The printed وز here is a fused form; none of the provided senses exactly match the conjunctive use, so its sense index is not available.) بفرمود is the honorific past “he commanded/ordered.” تا introduces a purpose/result clause (“that...”). گرگسار is a noun (apparently a place/name or epithet ‘Gorgasār’ — literally ‘wolf-place’ or ‘ferocious’); here it is the thing to be made the subject of the following clause.
segments.s391-g16: شود is the subjunctive/present-future form of شدن used after تا to mark the desired/result state: “may become / become / be made.” داغ دل is a compound meaning “burning grief of the heart” (داغ = burning grief/scar, دل = heart). پیش is the preposition “before/in the presence of,” so پیش اسفندیار = “before Esfandiyār.” Together: “that Gorgasār become (a) burning grief in the heart before Esfandiyār,” i.e. “that Gorgasār be made a cause of sorrow to Esfandiyār.”
segments.s391-g65: This clause places the means (به کشتی, 'by boat') up front, then the verb phrase بر او بگذرد ('passes over it'), with the subject شهریار ('the king') placed after the verb — a common poetic inversion. بگذرد is a 3rd-person singular present/simple narrative verb meaning 'passes' or 'crosses.' او ('it') is a pronoun referring back to the water/area just described.
segments.s391-g66: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; آید is 3rd-person singular 'comes.' به هامون marks destination ('to the hamun' — a marshy plain). ز بهر شکار is a purposive phrase 'for the sake of hunting' (ز here functions idiomatically with بهر to mean 'for').
segments.s391-g67: This temporal/conditional phrase begins with به indicating a temporal/durational sense and introduces صد سال‌گر — a poetic expression meaning 'a hundred years' with -گر functioning to give a hypothetical/conditional flavor ('if it were a hundred years'). ماند is the verb 'remained' and اندر حصار means 'within the fortress.' The clause thus reads 'if he remained a hundred years within the fortress.'
segments.s391-g68: ز هامون 'from the hamun' indicates origin/source; نیایدش is negative present 'does not come' with the enclitic -ش meaning 'to him' (i.e. 'does not come to him'). چیزی in negative context means 'anything' (hence 'nothing' in English) and به کار literally 'to (his) use' or 'of use.' The clause therefore says nothing from the hamun will be useful to him.
segments.s391-g69: هم‌اندر 'also/therein' + دژش 'his fortress' sets the location. کشتمند here functions as a noun/adjective meaning 'slayers' or 'deadly ones' (i.e. armed men/warriors) and the copula 'are' is omitted (typical in Persian poetry). و گیا adds 'and vegetation' — a collective noun for plants/greenery present within the fortress.
segments.s391-g70: درخت برومند pairs noun + adjective: درخت 'tree' and برومند 'thriving/prosperous' (here 'tall/lofty' or flourishing), describing prominent trees. و هم آسیا adds 'and also a mill'; هم intensifies 'also' and آسیا in this context is 'mill' (not the continent).
segments.s391-g71: چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'; اسفندیار is the subject. آن سخنها means 'those words' (آن + plural suffix -ها) and شنید is simple past 'heard.'
segments.s391-g72: زمانی here means 'for a moment/for a time' modifying the verb بپیچید, which literally means 'he twisted/turned' and is used figuratively to indicate a momentary change of expression or hesitation. و links the next action; دم is 'breath' and درکشید here is the literal sense 'drew in' (i.e. 'drew in his breath'), not the metaphorical 'understood.'
segments.s391-g89: به سان is a fixed simile marker meaning "like/as." گوزنان (plural of گوزن) is the comparison term "stags/deer." به سر بر سروی is a compact locative image: به + سر ("toward the head/with head") + بر + سروی ("upon/toward the cypress"); the phrase describes posture (heads raised toward a tall cypress) and has no explicit verb here because it is an adverbial simile modifying the following clause.
segments.s391-g90: همی is an aspectual particle giving habitual/continuous sense ("continually/always"). رزم شیران is a noun phrase where رزم ("battle") takes the following noun شیران as genitive/possessor ("battle of the lions/brave men"). کند is the classical/presentive verb "does/makes," and آرزوی ("wish/desire") is the object or verbal noun; the word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but yields "(he) continually makes/desires the battle of the lions."
segments.s391-g91: بفرمود تا is a reporting/ordering construction: بفرمود (honorific past "he commanded/said") + تا introducing a subordinate clause meaning "that/so that." همچنانش (= همچنان + -ش enclitic) means "him/it thus/still" or "as before." به بند is a prepositional phrase "into chains/fettered," serving as the complement of the order.
segments.s391-g92: به خرگاه بردند is a simple past clause: به خرگاه ("to the donkey-stall/low place") + بردند (past "they took/carried"). ناسودمند is a postposed adjective meaning "unprofitable/fruitless" (here qualifying the result or the act). The whole segment reports the action ordered above.
segments.s391-g93: بیاراست is a perfective verb meaning "he adorned/arranged." The direct object is یکی بزمگاه ("a certain/one banquet-hall") and خرم is an attributive adjective "joyous/lush." Word order is inverted poetically (verb before its object), but the structure is verb + object phrase.
segments.s391-g94: به سر بر نظاره is an instrumental/attitudinal phrase: به سر (here adverbial, "with (his) head") + بر نظاره ("for viewing/for the sight"). بر آن جشنگاه attaches the target: بر ("upon/over") + آن ("that") + جشنگاه ("place/occasion of celebration"). Altogether it means "with head raised, (he) looked at that place of festivity."
segments.s391-g95: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning "when." خورشید is the subject "the sun," and بنمود is the past verb "showed/appeared." تاج از فراز is a noun phrase where تاج ("crown") is qualified by از فراز ("from the heights/above"); the whole line reads "when the sun showed its crown from on high."
segments.s391-g96: هوا با زمین is a coordinated subject phrase "the air with the earth." نیز is an adverb "also/likewise." بگشاد is the past verb "opened/unveiled" (here causative/active perfective sense), and راز is the object "secret/mystery." The clause means "the air, along with the earth, likewise revealed/unveiled a secret."
segments.s391-g57: This is a simple nominal coordination: three items joined by و (and). سرمای سخت is a noun + adjective phrase (noun followed by adjective) meaning “severe cold.” There is no verb here; the following relative clause (introduced by که) will qualify these nouns.
segments.s391-g58: که introduces a relative/adverbial clause that qualifies the preceding nouns. چون here functions temporally (“when/as”), followed by the subject باد and the present-form خیزد (“rises/begins to blow”); the verb is 3rd-person singular present. به درد درخت is a prepositional phrase expressing the effect/result literally “to the pain of the tree” (i.e. causes the tree to suffer) — poetic, somewhat inverted phrasing.
segments.s391-g59: از آن پس is an idiomatic temporal phrase meaning “after that.” چو (here “when”) introduces a temporal subordinate clause. رویین دژ is a descriptive noun phrase (رویین = iron-hard, preceding the noun in this poetic register). آید پدید is a compound verbal idea: آید (“comes”) + پدید (“appears”) together mean “comes into sight / appears.” The clause is impersonal/narrative (no overt agent).
segments.s391-g60: This line contains two coordinated negative statements. نه … کسی … نه … places negation before the verb to deny any observer: نه … کسی = “no one …”. از آن سان is a prepositional/qualifying phrase meaning “of that sort / like that.” The first verb دید is perfective/past “saw,” and شنید in the second clause means “heard (of)” — together: “no one saw such a fortress, nor heard of it.”
segments.s391-g61: سر باره is a compound locative phrase (سر = top/summit, باره = load/thing upon it) indicating the summit or what lies over the top. برتر ز is a comparative construction “higher than / above,” with ز (from/of) introducing the standard of comparison. ابر سیاه is a noun + adjective (“black cloud”) describing that standard.
segments.s391-g62: بدو is a dative/locative clitic phrase “to it / upon it,” referring back to the fortress; در introduces location “in/within.” فراوان is an adverb/adjective “abundant” modifying the following nouns سلیح و سپاه (“weapons and troops”). The whole phrase is verbless descriptive continuation: “upon it, in abundance, arms and an army.”
segments.s391-g63: به گرد is a locative prepositional phrase “around,” followed by اندر + ش (اندرش) — archaic preposition + enclitic pronoun meaning “in/around it” or “about it.” رود (river/stream) and آب روان (running water) form a coordinated nominal phrase describing the water that encircles the fortress. روان here is an adjectival/participial use “flowing.”
segments.s391-g64: که introduces a result/cause clause (“so that / in that when”), here expressing the effect of seeing the fortress. از دیدنش is a causal/ablative phrase “from seeing it” (دیدن + enclitic ش = “seeing it”). خیره گردد is a predication “becomes astonished/stares” where گردد (from گشت) is present/subjunctive/presentive used as “becomes.” روان (here) refers back to the flowing water (personified) and is described as the patient/subject of that verb.
segments.s391-g49: سِپارت: "سه دیگر" = "three more / three other" (numeral + 'دیگر' for addition). "به نزدیک" is an adverbial phrase meaning "nearly / about" before the time-span, and "یک هفته راه" literally "a week's road" = "a week's journey" (هفته = week; راه = road/journey). Word order here places the approximative phrase before the time-span it modifies.
segments.s391-g50: "به هشتم" is a temporal phrase meaning "on the eighth (day)"; the following "به رویین دژ" is a locative/directional phrase "to the iron (hard) fortress". The verb "آید" is 3rd-person singular (present/historical-present) "comes/reaches", and the subject "سپاه" (army) follows the verb — a poetic inversion (verb before subject) is common in epic Persian.
segments.s391-g51: The first "پر" means "full (of)"; the clause reads as "it is full of lions and wolves" where "گرگست" is a contracted poetic form (گرگ + است) functioning as part of the predicate. The second "پر" again means "full (of)" and governs "اژدها" (dragon). The structure uses repetition of پر for emphasis: full of beasts and full of dragons.
segments.s391-g52: Relative clause introduced by "که" (that/which) modifies "اژدها": "from their claws no one finds release." "از چنگشان" = "from their claw(s)" with the possessive suffix -شان. "کس نیابد" uses "نیابد" (negative form of یابد) as 3rd-person present/historical-present meaning "does not find / cannot find"; under that negation "کس" functions as "no one." "رها" here means "release / escape."
segments.s391-g53: This is a coordinated nominal phrase: "فریب زن جادو و گرگ و شیر" lists sources of deceit — literally "the deceit of the woman, (of) magic, and (of) the wolf and the lion." "فریب" is the head noun (deception); the following nouns are coordinated with "و". In context this compound subject is what the predicate (فزونست) compares to the dragon.
segments.s391-g54: "فزونست" (فزون + است) is a present/epic-present predicate meaning "is more / exceeds." The comparison is marked by "از" + noun phrase: "از اژدهای دلیر" = "than the brave dragon." Word order places the comparative predicate before the than-phrase.
segments.s391-g55: "یکی را" is the direct object marker construction "one (accusative)." "ز دریا برآرد" = "brings up/from the sea" (ز = from; برآرد = bring forth/raise). "به ماه" marks the goal/direction "to the moon." The verb برآرد is 3rd-person singular (present/historical-present) and the subject is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s391-g56: "یکی را" again marks the object "one (another)." "نگون اندر آرد" is a resultative locative phrase: نگون (overturned/downcast) + اندر (into) + آرد (brings) = "casts down into." "به چاه" gives the destination "into the well/pit." Together: "he casts another down into the well."
segments.s391-g113: بدیدند is past simple 3rd-person plural 'they saw' (V + subject implied/known). The direct object follows: گرگان 'wolves'. بر is the locative preposition 'upon/on' and یال اوی 'his mane' gives the location. The conjunction و here is a poetic/linking particle inside the locative phrase (common in epic diction) rather than marking a new independent clause.
segments.s391-g114: میان is the preposition 'between'. یلی is an epithet 'valiant' qualifying the following noun (so 'the valiant Chang'). چنگ here functions as a proper/name or title; و links that noun to گوپال اوی, where گوپال is a name (Gopāl) and اوی is the possessive 'his'. The whole phrase = 'between the valiant Chang and his Gopāl.'
segments.s391-g115: ز هامون 'from the plain' is a simple ablative phrase. سوی او 'toward him' is the directional phrase. نهادند is past 3rd-person plural of نهادن 'they set/placed' and with روی ('face') functions idiomatically: نهادند روی = 'they set/turned (their) faces' (i.e. they faced/turned toward). The verb is plural past and the possessed faces ('their faces') are implicit rather than explicitly marked.
segments.s391-g116: دو is the numeral 'two' modifying پیل 'elephant'. سرافراز is an epithet after the noun ('proud/lofty'), a typical epic adjective placement. و links that phrase to 'دو جنگجوی' ('two warriors'). This nominal phrase apposes the previous clause (it names who turned their faces).
segments.s391-g117: کمان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker ('the bow' as object). به زه کرد is a poetic causative/compound: literally 'made (it) to the string' — i.e. 'drew the bowstring' — where کرد is the light verb in past 3rd-person. مرد دلیر is the subject 'the brave man' placed after the verb, a common VSO / inverted order in classical Persian.
segments.s391-g118: بغرید is past 3rd-person singular 'he roared'. بر سان is an idiomatic simile-maker where سان = 'like/as' and بر reinforces the simile ('like'). غرنده شیر is a descriptive noun phrase 'roaring lion' (غرنده modifying شیر). The whole segment = 'he roared like a roaring lion.'
segments.s391-g119: بر آهرمنان uses بر = 'against/upon' and آهرمنان (Ahriman) as the target; تیرباران is a deverbal/adverbial noun 'a shower of arrows' or 'arrow-rain'. گرفت is the past verb 'took' used idiomatically with تیرباران to mean 'he began/rained (a) shower of arrows'—i.e. 'he showered them with arrows.'
segments.s391-g120: به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with haste/with fierceness' (here 'with fierceness'). کمان سواران is a possessive noun phrase where کمان ('bow') is modified by the postposed possessor سواران ('the riders' / 'horsemen') — 'the riders' bows' — and گرفت repeats the past verb 'took/seized'. In epic Persian the same past stem گرفت can serve in contexts where later classical Persian would show explicit plural morphology, so the phrase reads 'the horsemen swiftly seized their bows.'
segments.s391-g137: Subject 'تو' + past tense verb 'کردی' (2nd person singular) begins the clause; the definite object is marked by 'را' after 'تن گرگ' ('the wolf's body'). The noun phrase 'خاک جای' follows as a predicative/result: classical word order allows the verb before its object and then a predicate complement meaning '(you) made (it) into earth/dust' (literally, 'made the wolf's body [be] earth/place').
segments.s391-g138: 'تو باشی' uses the present-subjunctive/optative sense of 'باشی' (a wish: 'may you be'); Persian omits a copula, so the predicate noun phrase 'رهنمای' (guide) follows the prepositional modifier 'به هر نیک و بد' ('in/for every good and bad [situation]'). Word order is: (subject) + optative verb + locative/prepositional phrase + predicate noun.
segments.s391-g139: The subordinating word 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when') and is followed by the simple past verb 'آمد' ('came'). The coordinated nouns 'سپاه و پشوتن' are the actors that come, and the adverbial 'فراز' ('on high/up') qualifies their arrival; classical Persian allows verb before some of its coordinated arguments and places adverbials after names.
segments.s391-g140: The finite past verb 'بدیدند' ('they saw') appears first, with its definite object 'یل را' ('the hero') marked by 'را'. The locative/prepositional phrase 'به جای نماز' ('at/in the place of prayer') gives the location in which the hero was seen. Word order (verb → object → locative phrase) is typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s391-g141: 'بماندند' is a past/plural verb here meaning 'they were left/they stood (astounded)'; 'زان' means 'from that' or 'by that' and introduces the cause. The noun phrase 'کار گردان شگفت' attributes the wonder: 'the wondrous deed of the troop' (here 'گردان' functions as 'troop' modifying or co-occurring with 'کار'). The whole phrase expresses that they were struck/amazed by that deed.
segments.s391-g142: Subject 'سپه' ('the army/host') is followed by the adverb 'یکسر' ('entirely, completely') modifying the verb phrase. 'اندیشه اندر گرفت' is a verbal construction meaning 'took/was seized by thought' (literally 'seized thought within'): 'گرفت' is past (3rd sg/pl depending on sense) used transitively here to mean 'seized' or 'took hold of', so the clause reads 'the host was wholly seized by thought'.
segments.s391-g143: The clause begins with 'که' introducing an explanatory or emphatic clause. 'این گرگ' is the noun phrase 'this wolf.' The form written as 'خوانیم‌گر' is a compacted/poetic piece: in classical usage a verbal form plus the conditional particle 'گر' can appear together (rendering 'if we call [him]'), and here the line lists epithets that follow ('پیل مست'). In this poetic stacking the speaker is saying, in effect, 'even if we call this wolf X or Y'; the sequence 'پیل مست' are two epithets: 'elephant' and 'drunk'.
segments.s391-g144: This is an optative wish introduced by 'که' and built with the adjective 'جاوید' followed by the optative particle 'باد' ('may it be'). The coordinated noun phrase 'این دل و تیغ و دست' ('this heart and sword and hand') is the locus of the wish: 'may this heart and this sword and this hand be eternal.' Such 'X باد' constructions are a common poetic formula for blessings/wishes in Persian.
segments.s391-g105: بدو گفت is a light prepositional + verb phrase: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) — literally “he said to him.” The following noun phrase لشکر به آیین بدار is reported speech: لشکر (army/host) + به آیین (to/into order/formation) + بدار here is an older or imperative-like form meaning “hold/keep” (stay in that state). Word order is normal: indirect object (بدو) before گفت, then the quoted/instructional noun phrase.
segments.s391-g106: همی پیچم is an older/poetic present/imperfective with همی marking continued or imminent action + پیچم (1st person singular of پیچیدن) = “I will/ I am going to turn” or “I shall turn away.” از governs the following noun (گفته گرگسار) meaning “from/away because of the said of the wolf-haunt.” گفته is a past-participial/nominal “said/report,” and گرگسار is descriptive—‘wolf‑haunted/place of wolves’ — so the whole phrase means roughly “I will turn aside because of what the wolf-haunt/territory reports (or because of the wolf-haunted place).”
segments.s391-g107: منم پیش رو‌گر is a self-identifying clause: منم (“I am”) + پیش (in front, ahead) + رو‌گر (a denominal/agentive, here ‘the one who goes forward/leader at the front’). The clause به من بد رسد uses به + indirect object (من) + بد (misfortune, evil) + رسد (present/past-subjunctive/optative meaning “befall/happen”) — literally “if misfortune happens to me.” The sequence expresses willingness to be the front man and to take any harm that may befall him.
segments.s391-g108: بدین کهتران بد نیاید سزد is a short conditional/wish/result clause with inverted order common in poetry. بدین (literally “by/with this” in prose; here introducing reference to ‘these’) + کهتران (the lesser/younger men) = “to these lesser men.” بد is the noun “evil/misfortune.” نیاید (negative present/subjunctive of آمدن) + سزد (“it befits/it is fitting”) form the evaluative result: “it should not come; it is not fitting.” So together: “it is not fitting that this evil come upon these lesser men.” Note the poetic inversion: the negated verb phrase نیاید سزد follows the object phrase.
segments.s391-g109: بیامد + بپوشید are two successive past-tense verbs describing consecutive actions: بیامد (he came) and پوشید (he put on/donned). In older Persian poetic narrative, verbs of motion and action are often juxtaposed without conjunctions. خفتان جنگ here functions as the object/complement of پوشید: “the garments/cloak of war” (i.e., war‑dress or battle‑garb); the precise noun خفتان is archaic/poetic but governed by پوشیدن “to put on.”
segments.s391-g110: ببست is the past tense ‘he bound/tied’ (not merely ‘closed’). از بر پشت is an older locative formula meaning roughly “around/on the back” (از/بر combine here to indicate placement on the back). شبرنگ تنگ is an adjectival phrase: شبرنگ (a variegated/patterned cloak) modified by تنگ (close‑fitting, tight). So the clause says he fastened a close, variegated cloak upon his back.
segments.s391-g111: سپهبد چو آمد is a temporal clause: سپهبد (the spahbed/commander) + چو آمد (chō = “when” + آمد = “came”) = “when the commander came.” به نزدیک گرگ is the prepositional phrase “to/near the wolf(s),” with به نزدیک functioning as “close to/near.” The whole segment sets the scene: ‘‘When the spahbed came close to the wolf…’’
segments.s391-g112: This is an exclamatory nominal clause introduced by چه (what/how — exclamatory) followed by a noun phrase in apposition: گرگ (wolf) — آن (that) + سرافراز (lofty/proud) + پیل سترگ (literally “elephant + سترگ”). The line uses metaphor and apposition: the wolf is likened to a magnificent/huge elephant. چه is an exclamative particle; the sequence is poetic and compresses appraisal into noun phrases rather than full clauses.
